question: Why does a DI container use Token<T> instead of string keys?

options.text: Token<T> binds the key to a type — resolve() returns a typed value, not any
String keys are not supported in Map
options.explanation: Map works perfectly with string keys. Token<T> is needed for type safety during resolve

options.text: Token<T> is faster than strings for container lookups
options.explanation: Performance is comparable. Token<T> is needed for compile-time guarantee of the correct type

options.text: It's a mandatory TypeScript requirement for working with generics
options.explanation: TypeScript doesn't require Token — it's an architectural decision for type-safe DI

question: What is the key idea of Ports & Adapters (hexagonal architecture)?

options.text: Business logic defines ports (interfaces), and the outside world provides adapters (implementations) — business logic doesn't know about DB, HTTP, etc.
Each module must have exactly one port and one adapter
options.explanation: A module can have any number of ports and adapters. The key is that business logic depends only on ports

options.text: Ports must always be asynchronous to support remote services
options.explanation: Ports are defined by business logic needs, not by adapter implementation details

options.text: Adapters must be written before ports
options.explanation: On the contrary — ports (interfaces) are defined first in business logic, then adapters are written

question: What is the dependency rule in Clean Architecture?

options.text: Dependencies point inward: Infrastructure -> Application -> Domain. Domain depends on nothing
Dependencies point outward: Domain -> Application -> Infrastructure
options.explanation: It's the opposite — Domain doesn't depend on outer layers. Infrastructure depends on Application and Domain

options.text: All layers depend on each other through interfaces
options.explanation: Dependencies are unidirectional — inward. Outer layers depend on inner ones, not vice versa

options.text: Domain depends on Application, Application depends on Infrastructure
options.explanation: This is the reverse direction. Correct: Infrastructure -> Application -> Domain

question: What problems do Module Contracts solve?

options.text: Isolation of module internals — other modules only see the public API
Freedom to refactor module internals as long as the contract is maintained
Preventing dependencies on internal utilities, constants, and helper functions of a module
Automatic generation of REST API from contracts
options.explanation: Module Contracts are a pattern for inter-module communication within a codebase, not for API generation

options.text: Replacing TypeScript interfaces with runtime checks
options.explanation: Module contracts use TypeScript interfaces, not replace them

question: Why shouldn't a port depend on adapter details (e.g., SQL or Postgres-specific types)?

options.text: Because a port is defined by business logic and must be abstract — this allows replacing the adapter without changing business logic
Because SQL queries can't be typed in TypeScript
options.explanation: SQL can be typed (Prisma, Drizzle). The problem is not SQL typing but violating the dependency direction

options.text: Because Postgres types aren't exported from the pg package
options.explanation: Postgres types are available. The issue is that ports must be abstract so business logic doesn't depend on a specific storage

options.text: To improve the performance of the abstract layer
options.explanation: Abstraction doesn't affect performance. The goal is architectural isolation of business logic from infrastructure
